title: Vegan flapjacks
subtitle: Chewy oat and golden syrup traybake without dairy
excerpt: A classic British flapjack made without butter: sunflower oil replaces the fat, and the golden syrup and sugar provide the binding sweetness. The result is a slightly chewier bar than a buttered flapjack, with a more neutral flavour that lets the oat and syrup come through clearly. Mixed in one bowl, pressed into a tin, and baked until just set at the edges.
sourceNotes: Flapjacks as oat-based traybakes sweetened with golden syrup are documented in British domestic cookery from the early twentieth century. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845, public domain and available at Project Gutenberg), describes oat-based baked items in the British tradition. The golden syrup and oat flapjack format is described in British domestic baking guides published before 1928, and oil-based variations appear as an adaptation in mid-twentieth-century British cookbooks.

content.text: The critical skill with flapjacks is timing the removal from the oven correctly. They need to come out while the centre still looks underdone: the
is edges-firm, centre-soft. Score into bars as soon as they come out of the oven while the mixture is still pliable, then leave in the tin to cool completely before lifting out.

Heat the oven to 180°C conventional (160°C fan). Line a 20 cm square tin with baking paper. Warm the golden syrup, sugar, oil, and salt together in a small saucepan over medium heat, stirring until the sugar dissolves and the mixture is runny. Do not boil. Remove from the heat and stir in the vanilla. Pour over the oats in a large bowl and mix until every oat is coated.

Tip into the prepared tin and press down firmly and evenly with the back of a spoon. Bake for 22 to 26 minutes until the edges are golden and set but the centre still appears slightly soft. Score into 16 bars immediately while still warm. Leave to cool completely in the tin before lifting out and separating.
